purpose: Classifying the current state of the fiscal doom loop and identifying monitoring criteria for transitions between dormant, slow burn, and acute phases.
components: Dormant: r << g, stable debt/GDP, no term premium pressure
Slow burn (CURRENT): r ≈ g, rising effective rate (~50-70bp/year), net interest ~$1T, three active stabilizers (reserve currency, T-bill management, foreign official buying)
Acute: r >> g, forced selling cascades, basis trade unwinds, political crisis (UK 2022 template: 4 trading days)
Monitoring criteria for transition: term premium acceleration, auction tail frequency, basis trade notional growth, foreign demand at auctions
current_assessment: Slow burn mode. Blended effective rate 3.3% rising toward 4.5-5.0% marginal rate. IMF cross-country base rate: 35-40% fiscal stress probability within 10 years. Can persist for years (Japan decades) or accelerate in days (UK 2022).
limitations: Japan analogy has structural differences (current account surplus, 90% domestic ownership, deflationary regime)
UK 2022 transition to acute was triggered by a specific policy error — hard to model systematically
Transition from slow burn to acute may be driven by events outside the fiscal domain (geopolitical shock, bank failure)
Framework does not model reversal from slow burn to dormant (requires sustained r < g)
